agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A production logistics system enforces a strict one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant with supervisor-gated reassignment. The product team requests an auto-split feature that would allow shipments over 500 kg to be divided among multiple carriers without supervisor involvement, directly conflicting with two established domain rules.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight to be delivered, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport provider assigned to fulfill a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept: a child Shipment created when a parent Shipment is split due to weight threshold. Does not yet exist in the domain model."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The act of binding exactly one Carrier to a Shipment. Once set, only a Supervisor can change it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role authorized to reassign Carriers on locked Shipments."},{"term":"Auto-split","definition":"Proposed behavior where the system automatically divides an over-weight Shipment into multiple sub-shipments, each with a different Carrier, bypassing supervisor approval."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is released for physical transport. The one-Carrier invariant must hold at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state-integrity constraint. If a Shipment exists without exactly one Carrier, or with multiple Carriers, the system is in a corrupt state — dispatch, tracking, billing, and liability attribution all break.","testable_assertion":"For any Shipment in any state from 'assigned' through 'dispatched', querying its Carrier must return exactly one result."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization policy enforcing operational control. A VP could change the approval gate (e.g., allow dispatchers to reassign under certain conditions), but the business chose supervisor-level control for safety.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to change the Carrier on a Shipment by a non-Supervisor principal must be rejected with an authorization error."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier assigned.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Dispatch without a single accountable Carrier breaks chain-of-custody, insurance liability, and delivery tracking. This is a physical-world constraint, not just a policy.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if the Shipment's assigned Carrier count is not exactly one."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The auto-split feature proposes creating multiple sub-shipments from one parent shipment when weight exceeds 500 kg, each with a different carrier, without supervisor involvement.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a proposed business policy (weight threshold, automatic action, no approval gate). The threshold and approval requirements are policy decisions, not physical constraints.","testable_assertion":"When a Shipment exceeds 500 kg, the system creates N sub-shipments each ≤ 500 kg, each assigned to a distinct Carrier, without requiring supervisor action."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Shipment with weight exceeding 500 kg is submitted","when":"The auto-split feature processes it","then":"The original Shipment is decomposed into multiple sub-shipments, each individually satisfying BR-1 (exactly one Carrier per sub-shipment)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Shipment is auto-split into sub-shipments","when":"Any sub-shipment reaches the dispatch stage","then":"That sub-shipment has exactly one assigned Carrier, preserving the dispatch invariant (BR-3)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Shipment is auto-split and sub-shipments are assigned to carriers","when":"A non-Supervisor user attempts to reassign a Carrier on any sub-shipment","then":"The reassignment is rejected per BR-2, unless the product team explicitly exempts auto-split sub-shipments from this rule"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Auto-split creates sub-shipments","when":"The parent Shipment is queried","then":"The parent Shipment's status clearly reflects that it has been split, and all sub-shipments are traceable back to the parent"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Shipment weighs exactly 500 kg or less","when":"The auto-split logic evaluates it","then":"No split occurs; the Shipment proceeds normally with one Carrier"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Shipment weighs 501 kg but cannot be logically divided (single indivisible item)","expected_behavior":"Auto-split must fail gracefully. The system cannot physically split an indivisible item. Must escalate to a Supervisor or reject with a clear reason."},{"scenario":"Auto-split produces a sub-shipment that itself exceeds 500 kg (e.g., 1200 kg split into 600+600)","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting or a smarter partitioning algorithm is needed. The invariant must hold for every sub-shipment."},{"scenario":"No available Carrier exists for one of the sub-shipments","expected_behavior":"The entire split must either be rolled back atomically or the unassigned sub-shipment must be queued — but it cannot be dispatched without a Carrier (BR-3)."},{"scenario":"Parent Shipment already has a locked Carrier assignment when weight is updated to exceed 500 kg","expected_behavior":"Splitting requires breaking the existing Carrier lock. Under current rules, this requires Supervisor approval — directly contradicting the 'no supervisor involvement' requirement."},{"scenario":"Customer disputes or insurance claims on a split shipment — who is liable?","expected_behavior":"Liability, billing, and chain-of-custody must be clearly defined per sub-shipment. The parent Shipment must retain an audit trail linking all sub-shipments."},{"scenario":"Shipment is split but one sub-shipment is lost or damaged in transit","expected_behavior":"Partial delivery handling must be defined. The parent Shipment cannot be marked 'delivered' until all sub-shipments are accounted for."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Incorrect splitting could lead to billing errors (who pays for each sub-shipment?), insurance liability gaps, and disputed claims. Proper implementation could unlock revenue from large shipments currently handled manually.","operational":"Auto-split reduces manual supervisor workload for large shipments but introduces complexity in tracking, reconciliation, and exception handling. Dispatchers and warehouse staff need to understand the parent/child relationship.","user":"Customers expect a single shipment — splitting must be transparent or clearly communicated. Multiple deliveries for one order changes the customer experience significantly.","risk":"HIGH — this feature directly contradicts two established domain rules. Shipping it without resolving the contradictions risks corrupting the Carrier assignment invariant, creating untrackable shipments, and breaking downstream systems (billing, tracking, insurance) that assume one-Carrier-per-Shipment.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their gatekeeper role on Carrier reassignment is bypassed for auto-split shipments. Must be consulted on whether this is acceptable."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"May receive partial shipments without full context. Contractual and SLA implications of handling sub-shipments vs full shipments."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"One shipment becomes multiple deliveries. Communication, tracking, and dispute resolution all change."},{"group":"Finance/Billing","impact":"Must handle invoicing for sub-shipments. Rate calculations may differ for partial loads vs full loads."},{"group":"Insurance/Legal","impact":"Chain-of-custody splits across multiple carriers. Liability attribution for damage or loss becomes complex."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Must introduce a new aggregate (parent-child Shipment hierarchy) without breaking existing consumers of the Shipment entity."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains two direct contradictions with established production invariants. The one-Carrier-per-Shipment invariant and the supervisor-gated reassignment rule are both explicitly violated by the proposed feature. Until the product team clarifies whether the domain model should evolve (e.g., introducing a sub-shipment concept that individually satisfies the invariant) or the rules should be relaxed, confidence remains very low.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradictions are real and clearly identifiable","The existing invariants (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) are well-defined","The auto-split concept requires a parent-child Shipment hierarchy to be domain-correct"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends sub-shipments to be first-class Shipments (each satisfying BR-1) or a new entity type","How billing, insurance, and liability should work for split shipments","Whether the 500 kg threshold is a hard rule or a configurable policy","What happens to the parent Shipment entity after splitting"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model is accurately described and enforced in production","The product team has not yet considered the invariant conflicts","Sub-shipments, if introduced, would need to individually satisfy the one-Carrier invariant to preserve system integrity"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature requires multiple Carriers per Shipment, directly violating BR-1 (exactly one Carrier per Shipment). The feature can only be domain-correct if a new 'sub-shipment' entity is introduced where each sub-shipment individually satisfies the one-Carrier invariant — but this is a significant domain model evolution that the product team has not acknowledged.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Supervisors","Carriers","Customers"],"options":["A) Introduce a parent-child Shipment hierarchy where each sub-shipment is a first-class Shipment with exactly one Carrier, preserving BR-1 at the sub-shipment level","B) Relax BR-1 to allow multiple Carriers per Shipment with a new 'multi-carrier' assignment model","C) Reject the feature as incompatible with the domain model"],"recommendation":"Option A — introduce sub-shipments as first-class domain entities that individually satisfy all existing invariants. The parent Shipment becomes a logical grouping. This preserves domain integrity while enabling the business capability."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature bypasses supervisor involvement for what is effectively a Carrier reassignment operation. When a Shipment already has a locked Carrier and is then split, the original assignment must be broken and new Carriers assigned — this directly violates BR-2 (only Supervisors can reassign).","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Engineering","Carriers"],"options":["A) Auto-split is treated as a 'creation' event (new sub-shipments), not a 'reassignment' — the parent Shipment is closed/voided and new sub-shipments are created with fresh Carrier assignments, sidestepping BR-2","B) Require supervisor approval for the split operation itself, but auto-assign Carriers to the resulting sub-shipments","C) Create a new authorization policy: system-initiated splits are exempt from supervisor approval"],"recommendation":"Option A or B. Option A is cleaner from a domain perspective — splitting creates new entities rather than modifying an existing locked assignment. Option B preserves the supervisor safety net while reducing their workload. The product team must decide which trade-off they prefer."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple Carriers to what the domain model considers a single Shipment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses Supervisor approval for Carrier assignment changes","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a locked Shipment"}]}

When a Shipment already has a locked Carrier and is then split, the original assignment must be broken and new Carriers assigned — this directly violates BR-2 (only Supervisors can reassign).","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Engineering","Carriers"],"options":["A) Auto-split is treated as a 'creation' event (new sub-shipments), not a 'reassignment' — the parent Shipment is closed/voided and new sub-shipments are created with fresh Carrier assignments, sidestepping BR-2","B) Require supervisor approval for the split operation itself, but auto-assign Carriers to the resulting sub-shipments","C) Create a new authorization policy: system-initiated splits are exempt from supervisor approval"],"recommendation":"Option A or B. Option A is cleaner from a domain perspective — splitting creates new entities rather than modifying an existing locked assignment. Option B preserves the supervisor safety net while reducing their workload.
